summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a new algorithm designed to measure whether regulatory agencies take public comments into account when finalizing rules. The model, developed in part by a former CFTC economist, uses NLP to score 'regulatory sentiment' from public comments. O'Neil argues the fundamental flaw is that industry lobbyists dominate public commenting systems both in volume and length of submissions, meaning the algorithm would primarily capture industry preferences rather than genuine public sentiment. She concludes this represents a mathematical model that could legitimize regulatory capture while appearing objective.
key_points: The algorithm measures regulatory sentiment on a -1 to +1 scale by analyzing word clusters in public comments
Industry insiders and lobbyists systematically overwhelm public commenting systems in both number and length of submissions
Aggregating all public comments into one score will inherently be dominated by well-resourced corporate interests
If such a model were adopted, it would create even stronger incentives for lobbyists to flood comment systems
Mathematical objectivity can mask deeply biased inputs and assumptions
money_quotes: What I do have a problem with is the apparent assumption that the 'the number assigned to all the public comments' makes any sense, and in particular whether it takes into account 'the public's view'.
The problem with this is that the industry insiders and their lobbyists overwhelm public commenting systems.
Now think about how the NLP algorithm will deal with this in a big pot: it will be dominated by the language of the pro-industry insiders.
If such a model were to be directly used, say to check that public commenting letters were written in a given case, lobbyists would have even more reason to overwhelm public commenting systems.
The take-away is that this is an amazing example of a so-called objective mathematical model set up to legitimize the watering down of financial regulation by lobbyists.
key_insight: Algorithms that treat all public comments equally will systematically amplify the voices of well-resourced lobbyists while creating a veneer of democratic legitimacy.
